county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1873 from Charles Mix County. Named for Stephen A. Douglas. Small agricultural county in south-central SD.
county_metadata.research_tips: Douglas County Register of Deeds in Armour holds deed records from 1873. BLM GLO homestead records at glorecords.blm.gov document original land entries. German-Russian (Mennonite and Hutterite) settlers were significant — check Mennonite church records at the Center for Mennonite Brethren Studies and Hutterite colony records. Douglas County Clerk of Courts in Armour has probate, naturalization, and civil records. The Armour Chronicle newspaper is on microfilm at SDSHS. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Czech and Scandinavian settlers also settled in the county. Hutterite colonies in Douglas County maintain their own genealogical records — contact colonies directly for access. Charles Mix County holds pre-1873 records for this area.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books from 1873 to present. Register of Deeds maintains index books and recorded instruments.
Marriage licenses and certificates from 1873. Register of Deeds issued licenses; state registration began 1905.

source_profiles.source_name: Douglas County Birth and Death Records
source_profiles.repository: South Dakota Vital Records, Pierre / Register of Deeds
source_profiles.coverage_notes: State registration from 1905; county-filed records may predate statewide compliance. Certified copies from SD Department of Health Vital Records. Some counties have earlier records filed with the Register of Deeds.

source_profiles.source_name: Douglas County Clerk of Courts Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate, guardianship, adoption, naturalization, civil and criminal case files from 1873. Clerk of Courts maintains court records for the circuit court.

source_profiles.source_name: BLM General Land Office Records — Douglas County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents for Douglas County including homestead, preemption, timber culture, and cash entries. South Dakota was entirely public domain — GLO records are the primary source for first land ownership.
Federal population schedules for Douglas County. Dakota Territory enumerated from 1860. Agricultural schedules (1850-1880) provide additional family and economic data.

source_profiles.repository: South Dakota State Historical Society / Chronicling America
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Historical newspapers from Douglas County communities. SDSHS holds the largest collection of SD newspapers on microfilm. Chronicling America has digitized SD papers.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1873. Named for Newton Edmunds, Dakota Territory governor. Agricultural county in north-central SD.
county_metadata.research_tips: Edmunds County Register of Deeds in Ipswich holds deed records from 1873. BLM GLO homestead records document original land entries. German-Russian settlers from the Black Sea region were predominant — check GRHS in Bismarck, ND and German Evangelical and Catholic church records. Edmunds County Clerk of Courts in Ipswich has probate, naturalization, and civil records. The Ipswich Tribune newspaper is on microfilm at SDSHS. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The county experienced significant population boom during the Great Dakota Boom (1878-1887) and decline during the drought and depression years. Railroad colonization brought many settlers; check railroad land sale records at the Minnesota Historical Society.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books from 1873 to present. Register of Deeds maintains index books and recorded instruments.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1883 from Custer County. Named for the Fall River. Hot Springs was a popular resort town known for its warm mineral springs. Home to the VA Hot Springs facility.
county_metadata.research_tips: Fall River County Register of Deeds in Hot Springs holds deed records from 1883. Hot Springs was a renowned health resort — the Hot Springs VA facility (1907) records at NARA document veterans from across the nation. BLM GLO homestead and mineral patent records cover original land entries. Fall River County Clerk of Courts has probate, civil, and naturalization records. The Wind Cave National Park area and Angostura Reservoir lands have federal records at NARA. The Hot Springs Star newspaper is on microfilm at SDSHS. Mammoth Site (paleontological) records document the area's scientific history. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Custer County holds pre-1883 records. The Black Hills and Pine Ridge Reservation border region creates complex jurisdictional history.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books from 1883 to present. Register of Deeds maintains index books and recorded instruments.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1873. Named for Andrew J. Faulk, Dakota Territory governor. Agricultural county in north-central SD.
county_metadata.research_tips: Faulk County Register of Deeds in Faulkton holds deed records from 1873. BLM GLO homestead records document original land entries. German-Russian and Scandinavian settlers predominated. Faulk County Clerk of Courts in Faulkton has probate, naturalization, and civil records. The Faulk County Record newspaper is on microfilm at SDSHS. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The county experienced typical Great Dakota Boom settlement (1878-1887) followed by drought-era population decline. German Evangelical, Lutheran, and Catholic church records serve the major ethnic communities. Check SDSHS for territorial-era records and land patent files. The county history book documents pioneer families and early community organizations.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books from 1873 to present. Register of Deeds maintains index books and recorded instruments.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1873 from Deuel County. Named for Ulysses S. Grant. Located on the Minnesota border in northeastern SD.
county_metadata.research_tips: Grant County Register of Deeds in Milbank holds deed records from 1873. Located on the Minnesota border, many families had connections in both states — check Grant County, MN records for related families. BLM GLO homestead records document original land entries. Scandinavian (Norwegian, Swedish) and German settlers predominated. Grant County Clerk of Courts in Milbank has probate, naturalization, and civil records. The Milbank Grant County Review newspaper is on microfilm at SDSHS. The Big Stone Lake area attracted early settlement. Milbank was a railroad town on the Milwaukee Road. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Lutheran and Catholic church records are essential. The county's granite quarries employed stoneworkers — industrial records at SDSHS.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books from 1873 to present. Register of Deeds maintains index books and recorded instruments.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1862 as original Dakota Territory county, reorganized 1898. Named for J. Shaw Gregory, Dakota Territory legislator. Opened from Rosebud Reservation lands.
county_metadata.research_tips: Gregory County Register of Deeds in Burke holds deed records; reorganized in 1898 when former Rosebud Reservation lands were opened. BLM GLO homestead records document settlers who filed claims on opened reservation lands. Gregory County Clerk of Courts has probate, civil, and naturalization records. The Rosebud Sioux Reservation borders the county — BIA records at NARA Kansas City include census rolls and allotment records for Sicangu Lakota families. The Burke Gazette and Gregory Times-Advocate newspapers are on microfilm at SDSHS. Dallas and Fairfax communities had significant Czech settlement — check Catholic parish records. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The 1904 Gregory County land lottery drew thousands of settlers — lottery records at SDSHS document successful claimants.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books from 1862 to present. Register of Deeds maintains index books and recorded instruments.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1914 from Stanley County. Named for King Haakon VII of Norway, reflecting the strong Norwegian settlement. Ranching country in west-central SD.
county_metadata.research_tips: Haakon County Register of Deeds in Philip holds deed records from 1914. Named for King Haakon VII of Norway, reflecting the county's Norwegian heritage — Norwegian Lutheran church records are essential sources. BLM GLO homestead records document original land entries. Haakon County Clerk of Courts in Philip has probate and civil records. The Philip Pioneer Review newspaper is on microfilm at SDSHS. Cattle ranching has been the primary economy; livestock brand records at the SD Brand Board identify ranching families. Stanley County holds pre-1914 records for this area. FamilySearch has some microfilmed county records. The Badlands border the county; early stockmen ranged cattle across open-range territory before fencing. The county experienced homesteader influx during the 1909-1918 boom period.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books from 1914 to present. Register of Deeds maintains index books and recorded instruments.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1873 from Deuel County. Named for Hannibal Hamlin, Lincoln's vice president. Lake county in east-central SD with Scandinavian settlement.
county_metadata.research_tips: Hamlin County Register of Deeds in Hayti holds deed records from 1873. BLM GLO homestead records document original land entries. Scandinavian (Norwegian, Swedish) settlers predominated — Lutheran church records are essential. Hamlin County Clerk of Courts in Hayti has probate, naturalization, and civil records. The Hamlin County Herald-Enterprise newspaper is on microfilm at SDSHS. Lake Norden and Lake Poinsett areas attracted early settlement. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The county's numerous glacial lakes were important for early transportation and water supply. Check Deuel County for pre-1873 records. Township histories document pioneer families and homestead settlement patterns.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1873. Named for George H. Hand, Dakota Territory lawyer. Agricultural county in central SD.
county_metadata.research_tips: Hand County Register of Deeds in Miller holds deed records from 1873. BLM GLO homestead records at glorecords.blm.gov document original land entries. German-Russian and Scandinavian settlers were prominent. Hand County Clerk of Courts in Miller has probate, naturalization, and civil records. The Hand County Press newspaper (Miller) is on microfilm at SDSHS. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The county experienced the typical Great Dakota Boom and subsequent drought-era population decline. Lutheran, Catholic, and German Evangelical church records are important sources. The county history books document pioneer families. Miller was a railroad town that served as a commercial center for surrounding agricultural communities.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1871. Named for Joseph R. Hanson, Dakota Territory pioneer. One of the earlier-organized counties in SD.
county_metadata.research_tips: Hanson County Register of Deeds in Alexandria holds deed records from 1871. As an early-organized county, Hanson County was the parent of several other counties — check SDSHS for territorial-era records. BLM GLO homestead records document original land entries. Norwegian and German settlers predominated. Hanson County Clerk of Courts in Alexandria has probate, naturalization, and civil records. The Alexandria Herald newspaper is on microfilm at SDSHS. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Lutheran church records are especially important for the Scandinavian settlement. The county was along early Dakota Territory transportation routes. Check parent-child county relationships when tracing records — Hanson was parent to Davison and other counties.
